title: Acmella occidentalis
taxonomy: Acmella occidentalis belongs to the family Asteraceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, such as its composite flowers and the presence of ray florets. Acmella occidentalis is also classified as being in the genus Acmella, which is a group of small, herbaceous plants that are native to the Americas. The genus Acmella is further divided into several species, with Acmella occidentalis being one of them. This classification helps to organize and understand the relationships between different plant species.
uses: Acmella occidentalis is a plant traditionally used in folk medicine, particularly in South America. Its leaves and flowers are known to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate pain and swelling. The plant's extracts are also believed to have antimicrobial and antioxidant effects. In some regions, Acmella occidentalis is used to treat various health conditions, including digestive issues and skin problems. More research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal properties and potential applications.
benefits: Acmella occidentalis has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits. Research suggests that it may have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which could help soothe and calm irritated skin. The plant's leaves and stems contain a compound called spilanthol, which has been shown to have analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. properties: Acmella occidentalis, also known as toothache plant, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its therapeutic properties. constituents: Acmella occidentalis contains sesquiterpene lactones, primarily 11-beta-hydroxy-11,13-dihydroacemellanin and acemellanin. The plant's active constituents are also reported to include flavonoids and phenolic compounds. These compounds have been associated with various biological activities, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. Additionally, the plant's sesquiterpene lactones have been shown to have potential antibacterial properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the active constituents and their effects on human health.

preparations: Acmella occidentalis is often used in herbal preparations for its reported benefits, which include reducing inflammation and pain. side_effects: Acmella occidentalis, also known as toothache plant, is not well-studied, but it is used in traditional medicine.
